359	- *Mechanical* — typeclass wiring, instance synthesis, ring-level algebra, simp/ring-tactic territory, definitional unfolding glue. A prover lane can comfortably close 3–6 of these in one iter (the attempt-cap permits each one a fresh budget). When the upcoming work is mechanical, load lanes aggressively.
360	- *Deep* — the load-bearing categorical / geometric / analytic argument. One per lane, often less. The prover may also legitimately spend an iter exploring without closing anything.
361	
362	Use this partition to decide how thickly to load each lane. Don't load a deep lane with three deep sorries; that just thrashes the prover's attempt budget across all three. Don't restrict a mechanical lane to one sorry "to keep it simple"; the prover wants the batched objectives.
363	
364	Balance difficulty so all provers finish around the same time. Avoid shallow / trivial objectives unless they unblock something downstream this iter. Don't artificially throttle — the prover prompt says "push as far as possible"; your objective list must give it room to.
365	
366	If a previous experiment is being restarted, check compilation status of every target `.lean` first. Prioritize files with sorries or compile errors; don't redo completed work.
367	
368	**Dispatch cap.** The runner refuses to fan out more than ~10 provers in a single iter (configurable via `--max-objectives`, default 10). Writing 15+ files into `## Current Objectives` is a planning failure, not a tooling limitation — even when the project has many open files, the right iter-level move is to pick the most urgent ≤10 (mechanical lanes counted) and defer the rest to the next iter. If the deterministic plan-validate guard truncates your list, the surplus is added to `USER_HINTS.md` so the next planner sees what got deferred. Don't rely on the safety net — pick the right ~10 the first time.
369	
370	**Blocked-deps filter.** Before dispatch, plan-validate also drops any objective whose transitive local imports failed the *previous* `lake build`. Reason: a prover assigned to `Downstream.lean` that imports `Upstream.lean`-which-doesn't-compile would fail to even load the file, burning API time for nothing. The blocked set is parsed from `.archon/last_lake_build.log`. There is one important exception: a blocked file that's *itself* an objective this iter is presumed-being-fixed — the planner is allowed to assign `Upstream.lean` and `Downstream.lean` together (the prover phase handles them in import order). When the filter drops files, they're listed in `USER_HINTS.md` with their specific blocking deps, so you can prioritize fixing the upstream files next iter. Best practice: when you see `## Build state` flagging compile errors, put those files at the top of `## Current Objectives` so the filter exempts the downstream lanes that depend on them.
371	
372	## Dependency graph
373	
374	Optional but cheap. Before scoping objectives, you may run:
375	
376	```
377	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=summary
378	```
379	
380	It parses imports + chapter `\lean{...}` / `\uses{...}` / `\proves{...}` / markers and emits a project-wide view in under a second. Use it to order objectives — upstream files first, downstream files later.
381	
382	## Stage transitions
383	
384	Advance `PROGRESS.md` when all current-stage objectives are met:
385	
386	- `autoformalize` → `prover` (all statements formalized)
387	- `prover` → `polish` (all sorries filled and verified)
388	- `polish` → `COMPLETE` (proofs clean, compile)

2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`):
6	nine protected declarations headlined by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
7	`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — existence of an Albanese / Jacobian
8	object uniform over the `k`-rational pointing of a smooth proper
9	geometrically irreducible curve `C / k`, with **no** `C(k) ≠ ∅` hypothesis.

17	genus; protected — cannot be re-typed).

66	Genus-0 arm = **Route C (Milne §I.3 rigidity)** — `J = Spec k` trivial,
67	char-general.

72	A.4 decomposed per table. A.4.a = dominant risk; A.4.b independently
73	startable.

3	## iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion (line 252; sorry at L437)
4	
5	### Attempt 1 (iter-186 Lane E)
6	- **Approach:** Refine the residual `appTop` chase by documenting the explicit
7	  mathematical content of the remaining gap, identifying the precise focused
8	  fact needed (`r_1.appTop(isLocElem) = 1` in `kbar`), and outlining the
9	  iter-187 closure plan via uniqueness of `r_1`.
10	- **Result:** PARTIAL — structural skeleton in place; residual is a documented
11	  focused `r_1.appTop(isLocElem) = 1` fact + the mechanical telescoping
12	  simp chain through `ΓSpecIso_naturality` + `pullbackSpecIso_inv_fst/snd` +
13	  `pullback.lift_fst/snd`.
14	
15	### Mathematical content of the gap
16	
17	After the existing Stages 1–7 (cancel `Proj.awayι(X 1)` via `congr 1`,
18	`Spec.map_comp` unfolding, cancel `Spec.map((homogeneousLocalizationAwayIso
19	kbar 1).toRingHom)` via `cancel_mono`, `ext_of_isAffine`), the goal is an
20	`appTop` equation between:
21	- LHS: `appTop(pullback.lift _ _ _ ≫ (4-step iso).hom ≫ Spec.map(eval₂Hom))`
22	- RHS: `appTop(Spec.map(algebraMap (MvPoly Unit kbar) (GmRing kbar)))`
23	
24	Both viewed as `CommRingCat` morphisms `Γ(Spec(MvPoly Unit kbar)) ⟶
25	Γ(Spec(GmRing))`.
26	
27	Conceptually: the section `s = iotaGm_chart1_section r_1 h_r_1` corresponds
28	via `(cover_X_iso 1).hom` to the unique `Spec.map(α)` for `α : Away_1
29	⊗[kbar] GmRing →+* GmRing` given by `Algebra.TensorProduct.lift` of:
30	- left component: `algMap kbar GmRing ∘ r_1.appTop : Away_1 → kbar → GmRing`
31	- right component: `𝟙 GmRing : GmRing → GmRing`
32	
33	The composition `eval₂Hom ≫ α` evaluates at `X ()` as:
34	```
35	α(isLocElem ⊗ algMap(X())) = α(isLocElem ⊗ 1) * α(1 ⊗ algMap(X()))
36	                            = algMap kbar GmRing(r_1.appTop(isLocElem)) * t
37	```
38	
39	For this to equal `algMap MvPoly→GmRing(X()) = t`, the focused identity
40	needed is:
41	```
42	r_1.appTop(isLocElem) = 1   in kbar
43	```
44	
45	### Why this fact is true
46	
47	`r_1 : Spec(kbar) → Spec(Away_1)` satisfies `r_1 ≫ Proj.awayι(X 1) =
48	onePt.left`. Since `Proj.awayι` is a mono (open immersion), `r_1` is the
49	unique morphism satisfying this; it equals
50	`IsOpenImmersion.lift awayι onePt.left h_range` (the canonical
51	construction used in `iotaGm_onePt_chart1_factor`).
52	
53	At the point `onePt = [1:1] ∈ ℙ¹` in chart 1 (where `X_0/X_1 = 1/1 = 1`),
54	the chart-1 evaluation map `Away_1 → kbar` sends `isLocElem = [X_0/X_1]`
55	to `1`. This is precisely `r_1.appTop(isLocElem)`.
56	
57	### iter-187 closure recipe
58	
59	Add a top-level helper lemma:
60	```lean
61	private lemma r_1_appTop_isLocElem_eq_one
62	    (r_1 : Spec(.of kbar) ⟶ Spec(.of Away_1))
63	    (h_r_1 : r_1 ≫ Proj.awayι(X 1) ⋯ ⋯ = onePt.left) :
64	    r_1.appTop ((ΓSpecIso _).inv (HomogeneousLocalization.Away.isLocalizationElem ⋯ ⋯))
65	      = (ΓSpecIso _).inv 1 := by
66	  -- Use cancel_mono on Proj.awayι to identify r_1 with the canonical IsOpenImmersion.lift
67	  -- construction, then compute via IsOpenImmersion.lift_appTop (if it exists) or
68	  -- via the explicit Proj.fromOfGlobalSections recipe from iotaGm_onePt_chart1_factor.
69	  sorry
70	```
71	
72	Then in `iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion`'s body after `ext_of_isAffine`:
73	
74	1. `simp only [Scheme.Hom.comp_appTop]` to break the chain into individual
75	   `.appTop`s in REVERSE composition order (Mathlib's `comp_appTop` is
76	   contravariant: `(f ≫ g).appTop = g.appTop ≫ f.appTop`).
77	
78	2. `simp only [Scheme.ΓSpecIso_naturality]` to convert each `Spec.map(?).appTop`
79	   factor into the `(ΓSpecIso).hom ≫ ? ≫ (ΓSpecIso).inv` form.
80	
81	3. The remaining iso factors (`pullbackSpecIso.hom`, `pullbackRightPullbackFstIso.hom`,
82	   `pullbackSymmetry.hom`, `pullback.congrHom.hom`, `pullback.lift _ _ _`)
83	   each have associated `appTop` simp lemmas (or telescope via
84	   `pullbackSpecIso_inv_fst/snd`, `pullbackRightPullbackFstIso_inv_fst/snd`,
85	   `pullbackSymmetry_inv_comp_fst/snd`, `pullback.lift_fst/snd`).
86	
87	4. After the telescoping, the goal reduces to a ring map equation
88	   `MvPoly Unit kbar → GmRing`. Apply `CommRingCat.hom_ext` to drop the
89	   `CommRingCat` wrapper, then `MvPolynomial.algHom_ext` (or
90	   `MvPolynomial.ringHom_ext`) to reduce to checking on `C r` and `X ()`.
91	
92	5. The `C r` case follows from algebra structure compatibility (both sides
93	   factor through `algMap kbar GmRing`).
94	
95	6. The `X ()` case is precisely
96	   `α(isLocElem ⊗ algMap(X())) = t`, which by tensor multiplicativity
97	   reduces to `r_1.appTop(isLocElem) = 1` (the helper).
98	
99	### What was tried this iter
100	
101	- Aggressive `simp only` with the option `backward.isDefEq.respectTransparency
102	  false` (the working idiom from `gmScalingP1_chart_PLB_eq`): the
103	  `pullback.congrHom` reduction fires, but downstream lemmas like
104	  `pullbackSpecIso_hom_base` do not match (the trailing `Spec.map(eval₂Hom)`
105	  is not `Spec.map(algMap kbar (S ⊗ T))`, so `_hom_base` doesn't fit).
106	- `apply Spec.map_injective`: fails because the LHS is not syntactically
107	  `Spec.map(_)` — would require first identifying the LHS chain as
108	  `Spec.map(α)` via pullback projections.
109	- `apply CommRingCat.hom_ext; refine MvPolynomial.ringHom_ext ?_ ?_`:
110	  the codomain is `Γ(Spec(GmRing))` not directly `GmRing`, so the metavariable
111	  inference fails on `Semiring`. Requires intermediate `ΓSpecIso` rewrite.
112	
113	### Negative lemma searches
114	
115	- `Spec.map_appTop` — does NOT exist in Mathlib (as standalone simp lemma);
116	  use `ΓSpecIso_naturality` instead.
117	- `pullback.lift_appTop` — does NOT exist in Mathlib; would need to be
118	  derived via `comp_appTop` and the iso chain.
119	- `IsOpenImmersion.lift_appTop` — does NOT exist; would need to be derived
120	  via `IsOpenImmersion.lift_fac` + `cancel_mono` on `awayι.appTop`.
121	
122	### Marker
123	
124	`iotaGm_chart1_composition_isOpenImmersion` — **NOT ready for `\leanok`**
125	(carries sorry). Blueprint: `chapters/AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex`
126	(no change needed; chapter already documents the chain).

19	## `IdentityComponent` (L129-133 → L133-160 after expansion)
20

23	  (connected component of `|G|` through `e`) → openness via EGA I 6.1.9
24	  → lift to `Scheme.Opens` → repackage via `Over.mk (U.ι ≫ G.hom)`.
25	- **Result**: **RESOLVED** (body no longer carries `sorry`; substantive
26	  content factored into the new private helper `identityComponentCarrier`).
27	- **Body**: `Over.mk ((identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom)`.
28	- **Key insight**: the abstract substrate is structurally
29	  `Over.mk` of `Scheme.Opens.ι ≫ structuralHom` — the substantive content
30	  (which open set, why it's open) all lives in the
31	  `G.left.Opens`-valued helper, freeing the `IdentityComponent` def

33	  inherit the structure morphism" idiomatically.

71	## `IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme` (L157-162 → L189-215 after expansion)
72	
73	### Attempt 1
74	- **Approach**: structured proof via `refine ⟨⟨Over.homMk ..., ?_, ?_⟩⟩`
75	  with explicit `(U := IdentityComponent G) (V := G)` annotations on
76	  `Over.homMk` to pin down the source/target types. Discharge the
77	  over-category compatibility `(identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom =
78	  (IdentityComponent G).hom` via `simp [IdentityComponent]` (closes
79	  because `IdentityComponent G = Over.mk (...)` so `.hom` simplifies via
80	  `Over.mk_hom`).
81	- **Result**: **PARTIAL — open-immersion half RESOLVED axiom-clean;
82	  closed-immersion half PARTIAL** (reduced to inline narrowly-named
83	  sorry `IsClosed (↑(identityComponentCarrier G) : Set _)`).
84	- **Open-immersion branch**: `change IsOpenImmersion (identityComponentCarrier G).ι`
85	  + `infer_instance` — closes via `Scheme.Opens.instIsOpenImmersionι`.
86	- **Closed-immersion branch**: `change IsClosedImmersion ... ι` +
87	  `apply IsClosedImmersion.of_isPreimmersion` (the open-immersion is
88	  automatically a preimmersion via `IsImmersion.instOfIsOpenImmersion`
89	  + immersion ⟹ preimmersion). Then `rw [Scheme.Opens.range_ι]` to
90	  reduce the goal to `IsClosed (↑(identityComponentCarrier G))`. The
91	  remaining inline `sorry` is the substantive Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3)
92	  paragraph 1 ("closure of a connected subspace is connected") applied
93	  to the carrier — gated on the same `LocallyConnectedSpace` /
94	  `connectedComponent` substrate as `identityComponentCarrier` itself.

97	  the carrier `Set`), not about scheme morphisms. This is downstream of
98	  the helper body close and should be axiom-clean once the helper lands.
99	
100	### Lemmas found

102	  preimmersion-plus-closed-range route to closed immersion. Used
103	  here as the closed-immersion-from-open-immersion bridge.
104	- `Scheme.Opens.range_ι` — collapses the range condition to membership
105	  in the underlying open set.

107	  `.left`-extraction step (used implicitly via `change`).
108	- `Over.mk_hom` — for the over-cat compatibility discharge.

112	  fails — the simp lemma uses pattern `(Over.homMk f w).left` but the

121	  doesn't immediately force unification with `IdentityComponent G ⟶ G`.
122	  Workaround: annotate `(U := IdentityComponent G) (V := G)` on
123	  `Over.homMk` explicitly.

54	     `(pullback g_C).obj ((pullback (pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N) ≅
55	     (pullback (g_C ≫ pullback.snd πC πT)).obj N`.
56	  2. `Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr key` where `key :
57	     g_C ≫ pullback.snd πC πT = pullback.snd πC πT' ≫ g` (via
58	     `Limits.pullback.lift_snd _ _ _` on the `pullback.map` lift).
59	  3. `(Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (pullback.snd πC πT') g).symm` —
60	     gives `(pullback (pullback.snd πC πT' ≫ g)).obj N ≅
61	     (pullback (pullback.snd πC πT')).obj ((pullback g).obj N)`.

64	    pullback f ≅ pullback (f ≫ g)` (Mathlib pseudo-functoriality of

66	  - `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr : (f = g) →
67	    pullback f ≅ pullback g`.

69	    k` — closes `key` because `pullback.map ... = pullback.lift

115	## Implementation notes
116	
117	- **`@[reducible]` on `OnProduct`** is what makes the downstream
118	  declarations work without explicit `show` / `change` unfoldings.
119	  Lean's typeclass machinery sees `OnProduct πC πT` and unfolds to
120	  `(Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules` for instance resolution (Category,
121	  Iso, etc.).
122	- **`Iso.app` (Mathlib `CategoryTheory.Iso.app`)** lets us extract
123	  the object-level iso from a natural iso between functors:
124	  `(F ≅ G).app X : F.obj X ≅ G.obj X`. Used twice in
125	  `pullback_pullback_eq` for the `pullbackComp` extractions.
126	- **The base-change morphism `g_C`** is built via
127	  `Limits.pullback.map πC πT' πC πT (𝟙 C) g (𝟙 S) _ _`. The two
128	  squares are closed by `Category.comp_id` / `Category.id_comp` and
129	  `hg : πT' = g ≫ πT`.

206

214	  object-level extraction from a natural iso between functors.
215	- `Limits.pullback.lift_snd : pullback.lift h k w ≫ pullback.snd
216	  = k` — the standard projection law on a pullback lift; closes
217	  `g_C ≫ pullback.snd πC πT = pullback.snd πC πT' ≫ g` because

239	  (the `set` keeps the `g_C` value transparent enough for `lift_snd`

244	  through a `def`). The `@[reducible]` attribute is what makes the

49	### Sub-task (b): `zero_mem'` (line 263 — RESOLVED axiom-clean)
50	
51	```lean
52	refine ⟨fun Q _ _ => ?_, fun _ => ?_⟩
53	· simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
54	· simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
55	```
56	
57	`Scheme.RationalMap.order Q 0 = WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 0) =
58	WithZero.log 0 = 0` is delivered by simp via `map_zero` and
59	`WithZero.log_zero`. Both `0 ≤ 0` and `-1 ≤ 0` are then trivial.

62	
63	The non-archimedean inequality on the discrete valuation of a
64	regular-in-codim-1 stalk (`Ring.ordFrac_add`, requires
65	`IsDiscreteValuationRing` on the stalk — supplied via iter-186 Step 1
66	class upgrade `IsRegularInCodimensionOne`).

68	Proof structure:
69	1. Helper `hMNZ`: `Ring.ordFrac _ y ≠ 0 ↔ y ≠ 0` (via
70	   `MonoidWithZeroHom.map_eq_zero_iff`, available on a field source).
71	2. Per-Q helper `key`: for any prime divisor `Q` and integer `n ≤ 0`,
72	   `n ≤ order Q a` and `n ≤ order Q b` ⟹ `n ≤ order Q (a + b)`.
73	3. Case-split `a + b = 0` (trivial), `a = 0` (reduce to `order Q b`),
74	   `b = 0` (reduce to `order Q a`), else use `Ring.ordFrac_add` +
75	   `WithZero.log_le_log` monotonicity + `min_cases` to split the
76	   `min` into a concrete branch.
77	4. Apply `key` with `n = 0` for `Q ≠ P` and `n = -1` for `Q = P`.

79	### Sub-task (d): `smul_mem'` (line 295 — RESOLVED axiom-clean)
80	
81	The `kbar`-scalar action `c • x = (algebraMap kbar K(C) c) * x` for any
82	`c, x` (via `Algebra.smul_def`). For `c ≠ 0` and `x ≠ 0`, the order
83	calculation reduces to `order Q (c • x) = order Q (algebraMap c) +
84	order Q x` (by multiplicativity + `WithZero.log_mul`).
85	
86	The non-trivial step: showing `order Q (algebraMap kbar K(C) c) ≥ 0`
87	for `c ≠ 0`. The lift `β := germ_at_Q (kToSection c) ∈ stalk_Q` is
88	nonzero (since `algebraMap c ≠ 0` and `α = algebraMap stalk K(C) β`
89	via `germ_stalkSpecializes_apply` functoriality). Then
90	`Ring.ordFrac_ge_one_of_ne_zero` on `β` gives `1 ≤ ordFrac (algebraMap
91	stalk K(C) β) = ordFrac α`. Applying `WithZero.log_le_log` (with
92	`(1 : WithZero (Multiplicative ℤ)).log = 0`) gives `0 ≤ order Q α`.
93	
94	Combined with `n ≤ order Q x` (where `n ∈ {0, -1}`), `linarith` closes
95	`n ≤ order Q α + order Q x = order Q (c • x)`.
96	
97	Two-case-split before the meat: `c = 0` and `x = 0` reduce to
98	`zero_mem'`-style trivialities.

104	- `Ring.ordFrac_add` (Mathlib `RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Noetherian`)
105	  — `min (ordFrac x) (ordFrac y) ≤ ordFrac (x + y)` on a DVR; needs
106	  `[IsDiscreteValuationRing R]`. iter-186 Step 1 class upgrade

108	- `Ring.ordFrac_ge_one_of_ne_zero` (Mathlib, same file) — for `x ∈ R`
109	  with `x ≠ 0`, `1 ≤ ordFrac R (algebraMap R K x)`. The key lemma for

111	- `WithZero.log_le_log` (Mathlib) — `x ≠ 0 → y ≠ 0 → (x.log ≤ y.log ↔

114	- `WithZero.log_mul` (Mathlib) — `x ≠ 0 → y ≠ 0 → (x * y).log =
115	  x.log + y.log`. Used to split `order Q (α * x) = order Q α +

160	    (P : C.left) (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1) :
161	    (TopologicalSpace.Opens C.left.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ ⥤ ModuleCat.{u} kbar where
162	  obj U := ModuleCat.of kbar ↥(carrierSubmodule P hPcoh U)
163	  map {U V} f := ModuleCat.ofHom <|
164	    Submodule.inclusion (carrierSet_mono P hPcoh
165	      (CategoryTheory.leOfHom f.unop))
166	  map_id U := by ext ⟨x, _⟩; rfl
167	  map_comp _ _ := by ext ⟨x, _⟩; rfl
168	```

280	/-- **The pole divisor of a morphism `φ : C → ℙ¹` of smooth proper curves
281	over `k̄`.** As a Weil divisor on `C`, it is `φ^*[∞]` — the pullback of the
282	divisor `[∞]` on `ℙ¹` along `φ`.
283	
284	The body is iter-183+ work; see `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin2.md` for the
285	construction route via affine-chart-localised `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia`
286	(Stacks tag `02RW` / `0AX5`). The substantive content — the assignment from
287	a morphism `φ` to its `[∞]`-pullback Weil divisor — is the named target of
288	`morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor`: the latter pins the existential to this
289	specific divisor (not just any positive-degree divisor on `C`). -/
290	noncomputable def Hom.poleDivisor
291	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
292	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
293	    [IsProper C.hom] [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
294	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
295	    (_φ : C ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar kbar) :
296	    C.left.WeilDivisor := sorry
297	
298	/-- **Degree identity for the pole divisor of a non-constant `C ⟶ ℙ¹`**
299	(Hartshorne IV §2 p. 299 opening + II §6.9 multiplicativity of degree under
300	finite pullback). The Weil-divisor degree of the pole divisor
301	`Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor φ = φ^*[∞]` equals the function-field-extension
302	degree `[K(C) : k̄(ℙ¹)] = Module.finrank K(ℙ¹) K(C)`, where the
303	`K(ℙ¹)`-algebra structure on `K(C)` is the canonical `φ`-induced one (the
304	typeclass binder pins this; per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md` Decision 2
305	convention).
306	
307	**Tier-3 honest sorry (iter-183).** The substantive body is iter-184+ work
308	via the affine-chart-localised `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia`
309	(Stacks `02RW` / `0AX5`) per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin2.md` Decision 2.
310	The strategy: pick an affine open `Spec A ⊂ ℙ¹` containing `∞`; the
311	preimage `Spec B ⊂ C` is finite over `Spec A` (non-constancy + smooth
312	proper curves ⟹ finite); both `A → B` are Dedekind extensions and
313	`Σ_{Q above P} e(Q|P) · f(Q|P) = [Frac B : Frac A] = [K(C) : K(ℙ¹)]`
314	identifies the pole-divisor degree with the function-field degree. The
315	non-constancy hypothesis is consumed in the finiteness reduction.
316	
317	This helper is the **single substantive gap** of
318	`morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` after the iter-182 plan-phase
319	signature strengthening: the wrapper body below is sorry-free assembly
320	that reduces to this helper. -/
321	theorem Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank
322	    {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
323	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]
324	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
325	    [IsIntegral C.left] [IsIntegral (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left]
326	    (φ : C ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar kbar)
327	    (_hφ_non_const :
328	      ∀ Q : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar kbar,
329	        φ ≠ toUnit C ≫ Q)
330	    [Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField C.left.functionField] :
331	    Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree (Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor φ) =
332	      (Module.finrank
333	        (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left.functionField
334	        C.left.functionField : ℤ) := by
335	  -- iter-186 Lane I scaffold for the `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` chain.
336	  --
337	  -- **The structural obstruction.** The right-hand side
338	  --   `Module.finrank K(ℙ¹) K(C)`
339	  -- depends only on the supplied `[Algebra K(ℙ¹) K(C)]` typeclass argument.
340	  -- The left-hand side
341	  --   `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree (Hom.poleDivisor φ)`
342	  -- depends on the body of `Hom.poleDivisor φ`, currently a typed `sorry`
343	  -- at L290 (the iter-186 directive defers this body to iter-187+:
344	  -- `Pin 3 body L482 and `Hom.poleDivisor` body iter-187+ separately`).
345	  -- Because `Hom.poleDivisor φ = sorry` definitionally, the LHS does NOT
346	  -- reduce; no `rfl`/`simp`/`rw` chain can substantively progress this
347	  -- goal without first replacing the def body.
348	  --
349	  -- The full proof chain (locked behind the `Hom.poleDivisor` body) is:
350	  --
351	  -- **Step 1 — affine chart at infinity.** Pick the affine open
352	  -- `Spec A ⊂ (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left` containing the point at
353	  -- infinity. Concretely `Spec A = (projectiveLineBarAffineCover kbar).f 1`
354	  -- (the chart `D₊(X 1)`, on which the standard ratio coordinate
355	  -- `t_∞ := X 0 / X 1` is a global function vanishing at the unique
356	  -- closed point `∞`). Mathlib API hooks:
357	  --   - `AlgebraicGeometry.projectiveLineBarAffineCover` (from
358	  --     `Genus0BaseObjects/BareScheme.lean`).
359	  --   - `IsAffineOpen.basicOpen` on the chart-1 affine open.
360	  --
361	  -- **Step 2 — preimage `Spec B := φ⁻¹(Spec A)`.** Non-constancy +
362	  -- smoothness of both `C` and `ℙ¹` over `k̄` ⟹ `φ` is finite (proper +
363	  -- quasi-finite via fibre dimension; Hartshorne II.6.8). The chart
364	  -- restriction `φ|_{Spec B} : Spec B → Spec A` is then a finite map of
365	  -- affine schemes, presenting `B` as a finite `A`-algebra. Mathlib hooks:
366	  --   - `AlgebraicGeometry.IsFinite.iff_isIntegralHom_and_locallyOfFiniteType`.
367	  --   - `IsAffineOpen.preimage` for the affine open preimage.
368	  --
369	  -- **Step 3 — apply `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia`.** With
370	  --   `R := A, S := B, K := Frac A = k̄(ℙ¹), L := Frac B = K(C)`
371	  -- and `p := (t_∞) ⊂ A` (the maximal ideal of `∞`), the Mathlib lemma
372	  -- `Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic.Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia`
373	  -- yields
374	  --   `∑ P ∈ primesOverFinset p S, e(P|p) · f(P|p) = [Frac B : Frac A]`.
375	  -- Required typeclasses (all derivable from the smooth-proper-curve
376	  -- setup + finiteness from Step 2): `[IsDedekindDomain A]`,
377	  -- `[IsDedekindDomain B]`, `[Module.Finite A B]`, `[IsFractionRing A K]`,
378	  -- `[IsFractionRing B L]`, `[Algebra K L]`, the scalar tower instances.
379	  --

2	\chapter{The rational-curve isomorphism \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) (RR.4)}
3	\label{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso}
4	\uses{def:genus, lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections, lem:degree_via_pole_divisor, lem:degree_one_morphism_iso, cor:nonconstant_function_genus_zero, thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero, def:codim1_cycles, def:divisor_degree}
5	% (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314)
6	% + IV \S2, opening of "Hurwitz's Theorem" (degree of a finite morphism of
7	% curves is the function-field extension degree; read from PDF page 316)
8	% + I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field \(\Leftrightarrow\) smooth-projective-curve
9	% equivalence of categories; read from PDF page 62). The verbatim quotes
10	% pasted in the declaration blocks below were copied character-by-character
11	% from the rendered page images of the scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
12	\section{Setup and motivation}
13	This chapter is the fourth and final sub-build chapter
14	\texttt{RR.1}--\texttt{RR.4} closing the project's headline \emph{RR bridge}
15	\[
16	  \mathtt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}
17	  \,\colon\,
18	  \text{a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with
19	  \(g(C) = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).}
20	\]
21	The chain assembled across the four sub-build chapters is:
22	\begin{itemize}
23	  \item \texttt{RR.1} (\texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}): the Weil-divisor
24	    group \(\Div(C)\), the degree map
25	    \(\deg \colon \Div(C) \to \mathbb Z\) (\texttt{def:divisor_degree}), and the
26	    degree-zero principal-divisor identity
27	    (\texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero}).
28	  \item \texttt{RR.2} (\texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}): the Riemann--Roch
29	    formula in genus zero \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\)
30	    (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}).
31	  \item \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter, dispatched parallel iter-174):
32	    the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\), its global-sections identification
33	    \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) = 2\), and the corollary
34	    \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} producing a non-constant
35	    rational function $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
36	    \bar k \cdot 1\( on a genus-\)0\( curve with a marked \)\bar k$-point.
37	  \item \texttt{RR.4} (\emph{this chapter}): the global classification ``a
38	    smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\)
39	    and a \(\bar k\)-rational point is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).''
40	\end{itemize}
41	This chapter consumes \texttt{RR.3} (the non-constant function) and produces
42	the Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} pinned at
43	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean} line~290 and restated as
44	the interface block \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
45	\texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}. The \texttt{RR.4}-closure of that target
46	also drops the \texttt{sorryAx} propagation tagged on
47	\texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme} via
48	\texttt{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}.
49	\paragraph{Scope of the \(\bar k\)-base classification.} The chapter restricts
50	to the case where the base field \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed. Over $\bar
51	k\( every closed point is \)\bar k\(-rational, so the ``\)C\( has a \)\bar
52	k$-rational point'' hypothesis of Hartshorne's Exercise~IV.1.3 is automatic
53	(the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) is non-empty,
54	hence has closed points, all of which are \(\bar k\)-rational). The general
55	``\(k \to \bar k\) descent'' step ``genus-\(0\) curve over a non-closed \(k\) with
56	a \(k\)-rational point \(\cong \mathbb P^1_k\)'' is a separate sub-build hosted
57	in \texttt{Jacobian.tex} (the \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key} downstream
58	consumer) and is out of scope here.
59	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is an
60	algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
61	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signature
62	(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) threads the
63	project's standing curve typeclass package:
64	\begin{quote}
65	  \texttt{\{kbar : Type u\} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]\\
66	   (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]\\
67	   \phantom{}\quad[SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]\\
68	   \phantom{}\quad[GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]}
69	\end{quote}
70	matching the typeclass set used by the headline target
71	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} in
72	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290--296}. The genus
73	\(g(C)\) is the one defined in \texttt{def:genus} of \texttt{Genus.tex},
74	namely \(g(C) = \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) via the project's
75	\(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology
76	(\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}); the concrete projective line target
77	\(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) is the project's
78	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveLineBar~kbar} of
79	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Genus0BaseObjects.lean}.

78	  - **STUCK** — no sorry-elimination or structural advance in K
79	    iters. STOP this route; address the blocker or pivot.
80	  - **UNCLEAR** — not enough signal yet (fresh route, 1-2 iters of
81	    data). Proceed but watch.
82	
83	  **CHURNING and STUCK are must-fix-this-iter.** The planner must
84	  respond — either with the action I recommend, or with an explicit
85	  rebuttal in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming why my read is wrong.
86	  Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the
87	  failure pattern this subagent exists to prevent.
88	---
89	
90	# Progress Critic
91	
92	You are the fresh-context progress critic. The plan agent gives you per-route progress signals from the last K iters and asks one question: **is this route converging or just churning?**
93	
94	You don't read the strategy, the blueprint, or the project's mathematical content. Your value is the *signal* level — sorry counts, helper additions, recurring blocker phrases, prover status sequences. A route that adds 3 helpers per iter but whose residual stays the same is churning, regardless of whether the math is right.
95	
96	## Stance
97	
98	You are the corrective for a known failure pattern. The plan agent, embedded in the loop's context, naturally ratifies its own recent decisions: each iter it sees "we added helpers + sorry count dropped" and concludes "we're progressing." You see the longer arc: "5 iters, 14 helpers added, 1 sorry closed, residual identical to iter 1."
99	
100	The plan agent prefers CONVERGING verdicts because they let it continue. You should NOT give that bias the benefit of the doubt. When the signals point at churn, you say CHURNING. When the signals point at stall, you say STUCK. You don't soften.
101	
102	**The plan agent's laziness is also a signal.** The following planner behaviors are themselves churn patterns — they are not "good planning" and must not be treated as structural progress:
103	
104	- Dispatching a single prover when multiple files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries are available and could be worked on at the same time. Under-loaded dispatch is artificial throttling and burns iterations.
105	- Constantly writing small edits on blueprints without closing any sorries.
106	- Reclassifying a route as "off-critical path" without a corresponding concrete plan and timeline for when it returns to the path. "Off-critical path" with no re-engagement plan is indefinite deferral.
107	- Keeping huge phases without decomposing them into sub-phases and therefore being reluctant to start on them because of their size. 
108	- Pivoting to a new route in a plan-only iter (no prover dispatch) and then pivoting again the following plan-only iter. Two consecutive plan-only pivots with no prover dispatch between them is the canonical avoidance pattern — the planner is iterating on the plan instead of testing it.
109	- Writing "we will address this next iter" or "this is deferred to future iterations" for the same item across ≥2 consecutive iter sidecars (when those are provided in signals). Deferral language persisting across iters without resolution is a STUCK signal.
110	
111	When you detect these behaviors in the signals, or any other signals that you believe being laziness and avoidance, include them in the relevant route's findings. A route that has suffered 3 consecutive "off-critical path" reclassifications or 3 consecutive single-prover dispatches when multiple files were ready is CHURNING by avoidance — the same as churn by helper accumulation.
112	
113	## Directive Format
114	
115	```markdown

117

119	<slug>
120

120

127

129

132

134	- iter-NNN-K: <count> (e.g. 5)
135	- iter-NNN-(K-1): <count> (e.g. 5)
136	- iter-NNN-(K-2): <count> (e.g. 4)

144

146	- iter-NNN-K: COMPLETE | PARTIAL | INCOMPLETE — <one-line summary from prover report>
147	- ...
148	- iter-NNN-1: COMPLETE | PARTIAL | INCOMPLETE — <one-line summary>
149

152	- ...
153	- iter-NNN-1: <N files dispatched>
154

156	is preferred — it exposes under-dispatch directly.)
157

160	- ...

164	- ...

168	- ...

172	- **Elapsed iters in current phase**: <e.g. "9">
173	- **Phase started at iter**: <e.g. "iter-117">
174	
175	#### Planner's current proposal for this iter
176	- <one paragraph: what the planner wants to assign>
177

179

181

183	- **Files**: <comma-separated basenames>
184	- **Files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries (ready but not dispatched)**: <list or "none identified">

186

188	<routes the planner is NOT considering this iter and does not want assessed>
189	```
190	
191	## What you check
192	
193	For each route's block:
194	
195	1. **Sorry trajectory.** Is the count actually dropping over the K-iter window? "Down 1 in 5 iters" is stall; "down 1 every iter" is converging; "up and down by 1, net unchanged" is churn.
196	
197	2. **Helper accumulation vs payoff.** If helpers are being added but the residual doesn't shrink, that's churn. "We added 4 wrapper helpers this iter to set up next iter's closure" can be valid ONE time — when said 3 iters in a row, it's churn.
198	
199	3. **Recurring blockers.** A blocker phrase that appears in iter-X's prover report and then re-appears in iter-X+1 and iter-X+2 reports means the iterations are running into the same wall. That's STUCK.
200	
201	4. **Prover status pattern.** COMPLETE → COMPLETE → COMPLETE is converging. PARTIAL → PARTIAL → PARTIAL is churn. INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is stuck. PARTIAL → INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is regressing.
202	
203	5. **Under-dispatch pattern.** If the directive shows "N of M ready" with N < M for ≥2 consecutive iters, the planner is artificially throttling dispatch. This is churn by avoidance — not a prover problem but a planning problem. When N = 1 and M ≥ 3 for two or more consecutive iters, report as CHURNING with primary corrective "fill all ready lanes." Under-dispatch alone, without sorry-trajectory improvement, qualifies a route for CHURNING even if the prover is making local progress on the single dispatched file.
204	
205	6. **Avoidance patterns.** Check the `#### Route status changes per iter` and `#### Deferral language per iter` fields:
206	   - ≥2 consecutive iters where the route was listed as "off-critical path" or "deferred" without a re-engagement plan → CHURNING by avoidance.
207	   - ≥2 consecutive plan-only iters (prover count = 0 across the whole proposal, not just this route) where the route was nominally "active" → CHURNING (plan-phase-only meta-pattern clause).
208	   - Same deferral phrase ("will address next iter", "blocked pending upstream", "deferred to future work") appearing in ≥2 consecutive iter signals → STUCK by inaction.
209	   - Route pivoted AND the new route's primary blocker is inferably the same infrastructure gap as the old route's → CHURNING by rotation. (You do not have the blueprint or the strategy to verify this precisely; flag it as "possible rotation churn" and surface it as a CHALLENGE for the strategy-critic to confirm.)
210	
211	7. **PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity.** Independent of the route-level verdict, sanity-check the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal:
212	
213	   - **Over the dispatch cap**: if file count > the cap shown in the directive (default 10), this is an automatic CHURNING-equivalent finding regardless of route convergence. Runaway fan-out (e.g. 27 provers in one iter) is the failure mode this check exists to prevent.
214	   - **Under-dispatch against ready files**: if the proposal lists fewer files than the "Files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries (ready but not dispatched)" field shows are available, flag the gap explicitly. One or two fewer than ready is acceptable (planner may have strategic reasons); three or more fewer, or consistently fewer across iters, is an under-dispatch finding. Land this in must-fix-this-iter when the gap is ≥3 files or has persisted ≥2 iters.
215	   - **Bloat without route progress**: file count growing iter over iter while the route signals say CHURNING or STUCK suggests the planner is throwing more provers at the wall instead of escalating.
216	
217	   These are dispatch-level checks, not route-level. They land in a separate "PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity" block in your report.
218	
219	8. **Throughput honesty.** Compare `Iters left` (verbatim from STRATEGY.md's `## Phases & estimations`) against elapsed iters in the current phase. Bucket:
220	
221	   - **On schedule**: elapsed ≤ estimate.
222	   - **Slipping**: elapsed > estimate but ≤ 2× estimate.
223	   - **Over budget**: elapsed > 2× estimate.
224	   - **Estimate-free**: STRATEGY.md gives no number, or "?", for this row.
225	
226	   "Over budget" with a still-positive `Iters left` is the dishonest-estimate signature. "Estimate-free" with elapsed > 5 iters in a phase is also flag-worthy.
227	
228	## Verdict rules
229	
230	Apply these rules verbatim:
231	
232	- **CONVERGING**: sorry count strictly decreasing in K-iter window AND no recurring blocker AND no under-dispatch pattern AND no avoidance pattern AND planner's proposal looks like "finish what's started."
233	- **CHURNING**: any of the following:
234	  - helpers added in ≥2 of last K iters AND sorry count net unchanged or down by <1 per 2 iters AND no structural change in approach;
235	  - PARTIAL prover status ≥3 of last K iters;
236	  - **plan-phase-only meta-pattern**: ≥3 consecutive iters with zero prover dispatches on this route (no file ever appearing in `## Current Objectives`). Pure planning rounds without ever firing a prover is textbook stall.
237	  - **under-dispatch pattern**: prover count = 1 (or otherwise N < M ready) for ≥2 consecutive iters with no sorry-trajectory improvement attributable to the dispatch strategy (i.e. the filed sorry on the dispatched lane didn't close either).
238	  - **avoidance pattern**: ≥2 consecutive iters with "off-critical path" / "deferred" route status AND no re-engagement plan in the proposal.
239	- **STUCK**: sorry count unchanged across K iters AND prover statuses include INCOMPLETE OR recurring blocker phrase across ≥3 iters. OR: helpers added without any sorry-elimination across K iters. OR: same deferral phrase persisting across ≥2 consecutive iters.

10	/-!
11	# The rational-curve isomorphism `C ≅ ℙ¹` (RR.4)
12	
13	This file is the **RR.4** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
14	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically irreducible
15	genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma pinned in
16	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity:290`).
17	
18	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain for the genus-`0` ⇒ `ℙ¹` classification routes
19	through the four sub-build chapters:
20	
21	- `RR.1` (`WeilDivisor.lean`): the Weil divisor group `Div(C)`, the degree
22	  map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ`, and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity.
23	- `RR.2` (`RRFormula.lean`): the Euler-characteristic identity
24	  `χ(𝒪_C(D)) = deg(D) + 1 − g` and Riemann–Roch in genus zero.
25	- `RR.3` (`OCofP.lean`): the line bundle `𝒪_C([P])` of a closed point and
26	  the existence of a non-constant rational function
27	  `f ∈ H⁰(C, 𝒪_C([P])) ∖ k̄ · 1`.
28	- **`RR.4` (this file)**: the global classification — the linear-system
29	  morphism `C ⟶ ℙ¹` produced from `(1, f)` (Pin 1), its degree identified
30	  via the pole divisor (Pin 2), and the degree-`1` ⇒ isomorphism step
31	  (Pin 3). The headline target `AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1`
32	  (Pin 4) is the existing pinned declaration at
33	  `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290` — see §0 below.
34	
35	## Status (iter-177 file-skeleton; iter-181 Pin 3 signature refinement)
36	
37	This file was originally the **iter-177 Lane 8** file-skeleton. Each
38	of the three new pinned declarations carries the *intended*
39	substantive type signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
40	`blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.tex`) with a
41	`sorry` body. Bodies are iter-182+ work after the RR.1/RR.2/RR.3
42	chain matures (in particular `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero`
43	and the `H⁰(C, 𝒪_C([P]))`-non-constant existence corollary of `RR.3`).
44	
45	iter-181 Lane I refined the Pin 3 signature of
46	`iso_of_degree_one`: the abstract function-field-iso existence
47	hypothesis is replaced by the `[Algebra K(C') K(C)]` typeclass binder
48	+ `Module.finrank K(C') K(C) = 1` equation, matching what the
49	iter-182+ body needs (per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`
50	Decision 2 DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY).
51	
52	The 3 new pinned declarations are:
53	
54	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections` — from a
55	   graded `k̄`-algebra hom `k̄[X₀, X₁] →+* Γ(X, ⊤)` whose image of the
56	   irrelevant ideal generates the unit ideal, produce the morphism
57	   `X ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄` via `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections` lifted to
58	   the slice category over `Spec k̄`.
59	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` — for a
60	   non-constant morphism `φ : C ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄` from a smooth
61	   proper curve, extract a positive-degree Weil divisor on `C` (the
62	   pole divisor `φ^*[∞]`).
63	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one` — a non-constant
64	   morphism between smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves
65	   whose induced function-field map is a `k̄`-algebra iso is an
66	   isomorphism of schemes (Hartshorne I.6.12 specialised to
67	   degree-`1`).
68	
69	The headline pin (Pin 4) is satisfied by the existing AVR.lean target.
70	
71	## Note on type expressivity
72	
73	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
74	each pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
75	
76	* `morphismToP1OfGlobalSections` returns a *concrete* morphism
77	  `X ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar k̄` of `Over (Spec k̄)`, not `Iso.refl _` or a
78	  reflexive placeholder; the input data is the exact pair
79	  `(f, hf)` consumed by Mathlib's `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections`.
80	* `morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor` asserts the existence of a
81	  positive-integer degree `d` together with a Weil divisor `D` on `C`
82	  realising it — the *named substantive content* of the degree-via-pole
83	  identification. Unfolds to `∃ d D, 0 < d ∧ D.degree = d`, not `True`.
84	* `iso_of_degree_one` asserts `Nonempty (C ≅ C')` from the
85	  non-constant hypothesis together with the *substantive* degree-`1`
86	  hypothesis `Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1`
87	  (paired with an `[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]` typeclass
88	  binder for the canonical `φ`-induced function-field map). The
89	  `Nonempty`-wrapped iso is the exact downstream-consumer shape,
90	  matching the `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` headline signature in AVR.lean.
91	  This signature was refined in iter-181 Lane I from the iter-177
92	  file-skeleton placeholder `Nonempty (C'.ff ≃+* C.ff)` to make the
93	  degree-`1`-induced-by-`φ` hypothesis the substantive content; see
94	  `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md` Decision 2.
95	
96	Unfolding any pinned declaration exposes the named substantive content
97	(the concrete `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections`-derived morphism, the
98	existence-of-positive-degree-divisor statement, the existence of a
99	scheme isomorphism); no `Iso.refl _` / empty-content `proof_wanted` /
100	`Classical.choice ⟨witness⟩` placeholders are used.

259	/-! ## §2. Pin 2 — the degree of a non-constant `C ⟶ ℙ¹` via its pole divisor
260	(`morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor`)
261	
262	Hartshorne II §6 + IV §2 opening: for a non-constant morphism
263	`φ : C ⟶ ℙ¹` from a smooth proper curve, the function-field extension
264	`k̄(ℙ¹) ↪ K(C)` is finite, and the degree
265	`[K(C) : k̄(ℙ¹)] = deg(φ)` is recovered as the degree of the pullback
266	of any closed point of `ℙ¹` to `C`. Specialising to `Q = ∞`, this
267	identifies `deg(φ)` with the degree of the **pole divisor** of the
268	pulled-back affine coordinate `φ^* u`.
269	
270	For the iter-177 file-skeleton the substantive content is the
271	existence of a positive-degree Weil divisor on `C` realising
272	`deg(φ)`; the body in iter-178+ produces this divisor as the pole
273	divisor of `φ^* u` via the Hartshorne II.6.9 multiplicativity-of-degree
274	identity.
275	
276	This is a *separable* sub-build pin (the lemma is stated independently
277	of the headline `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` and does not require `g(C) = 0`).
278	-/
279	
280	/-- **The pole divisor of a morphism `φ : C → ℙ¹` of smooth proper curves
281	over `k̄`.** As a Weil divisor on `C`, it is `φ^*[∞]` — the pullback of the
282	divisor `[∞]` on `ℙ¹` along `φ`.
283	
284	The body is iter-183+ work; see `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin2.md` for the
285	construction route via affine-chart-localised `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia`
286	(Stacks tag `02RW` / `0AX5`). The substantive content — the assignment from
287	a morphism `φ` to its `[∞]`-pullback Weil divisor — is the named target of
288	`morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor`: the latter pins the existential to this
289	specific divisor (not just any positive-degree divisor on `C`). -/

237	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
238	
239	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
240	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
241	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
242	
243	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
244	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
245	
246	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
247	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
248	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
249	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
250	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
251	
252	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).

254	iter-174 body: the function is junk-defined outside its intended scope. We
255	case-split on `Order.coheight P = 1` (the codimension-one witness of
256	`PrimeDivisor`). On the branch where the equality holds — automatic for a
257	closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme — we promote `P` to a
258	`PrimeDivisor` via the witness and return `Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1`, i.e.
259	the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one. On the off-branch (junk regime)
260	we return the zero divisor. The blueprint pins the well-definedness argument
261	"`IsClosed {P}` on a one-dimensional integral scheme ⟹ `coheight P = 1`" as a
262	separate threadable hypothesis at the call site (chapter L330–L340 "Lean
263	signature scope"); see `ofClosedPoint_eq_single` for the equation in the
264	hypothesised regime. -/
265	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
266	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
267	  if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0
268	
269	/-- In the hypothesised regime where the closed point `P` has coheight one
270	(the codim-1 condition automatic for a closed point of a one-dimensional
271	integral scheme), `ofClosedPoint P hP` is the prime divisor `P` with
272	multiplicity one. -/
273	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_single {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
274	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P = 1) :
275	    ofClosedPoint P hP = Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 := by
276	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
277	
278	/-- Off-branch: outside the codim-1 regime, `ofClosedPoint` is junk-defined as
279	the zero divisor. (Only relevant when the user supplies a "closed point" that
280	does not have coheight one in the ambient scheme — e.g. a generic point or a
281	codim-≥2 point on a higher-dimensional scheme.) -/
282	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_zero {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
283	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P ≠ 1) :
284	    ofClosedPoint P hP = 0 := by
285	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]

289	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
290	
291	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
292	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
293	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
294	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.
295	
296	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
297	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge
298	needs only the `k̄` specialisation.)
299	
300	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
301	
302	Implementation: `Finsupp.sum D (fun _ n => n)` is the sum of all coefficients of
303	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
304	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
305	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)
306	
307	/-- **The degree map is a group homomorphism `Div(C) → ℤ`.**
308

117	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
118	
119	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
120	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
121	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
122	
123	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
124	
125	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
126	
127	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
128	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
129	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
130	discrete valuation on `f`.
131	
132	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
133	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
134	
135	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
136	
137	iter-176 body (per analogist `dvr-rationalmap-order`): the body uses Mathlib's
138	`Ring.ordFrac` (the canonical `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom from
139	`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`, Stacks `02MD`) on the stalk
140	`X.presheaf.stalk Y.point`, then projects through `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
141	(the canonical projection with junk-on-zero, `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero`).
142	On `f = 0` this gives `order Y 0 = 0` (junk convention from `WithZero.log_zero`).
143	
144	The required Mathlib typeclasses on the stalk are:
145	- `IsNoetherianRing` — from `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`.
146	- `IsDomain` ⟹ `Nontrivial` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
147	- `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
148	- `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` — threaded explicitly (the
149	  topological-coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge `Order.coheight Y.point = 1 ⟹
150	  Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` is a Mathlib-upstream-pending
151	  gap; see Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). -/
152	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
153	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
154	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
155	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
156	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)
157	
158	end Scheme.RationalMap

173	`O_{X,Y.point}`. The `[IsIntegral X]` precondition makes

177	class Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne (X : Scheme.{u}) [IsIntegral X] : Prop where

41	  `IsRegularLocalRing.instOfIsLocalRingOfIsDomainOfIsPrincipalIdealRing`
42	  (`Defs.lean:68–76`) — i.e. `IsLocalRing + IsDomain + IsPrincipalIdealRing
43	  → IsRegularLocalRing`. There is **no** declaration whose conclusion is
44	  `IsDomain R` from `IsRegularLocalRing R`.
45	- **Direct LSP verification**: `lean_loogle "IsRegularLocalRing, IsDomain"`
46	  returns exactly the one converse-direction hit above; `lean_leansearch
47	  "regular local ring is a domain"` surfaces no relevant statements;
48	  `grep -rln "IsRegularLocalRing" Mathlib` returns the single
49	  `Defs.lean` file.
50	- **Project's current path**: typed `sorry` body in
51	  `exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal`. The iter-181 task_result already
52	  identified this as a Mathlib gap.
53	- **Gap**: divergent — the project needs the implication but Mathlib does
54	  not provide it. Not a parallel-API issue; it is a genuinely missing
55	  upstream theorem.
56	- **Verdict**: **NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL**.
57	
58	### Decision 2: If absent, which 00NQ route is cheapest at pinned commit `b80f227`?
59	
60	The Stacks 00NQ classical proof is a *joint induction* on
61	`(maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank` proving simultaneously `IsRegularLocalRing
62	R ⟹ IsDomain R` and the existence of an `R`-regular sequence of length
63	`spanFinrank` inside `𝔪`. Sub-lemmas and their Mathlib status:
64	
65	| Sub-lemma | Mathlib citation | LOC to assemble |
66	|---|---|---|
67	| Base case: `spanFinrank = 0 ⟹ 𝔪 = ⊥ ⟹ R is a field ⟹ IsDomain R` | `IsLocalRing.isField_iff_maximalIdeal_eq` + `Field.toIsDomain` (or `Submodule.spanFinrank_eq_zero_iff`) | 25 |
68	| Finitely many minimal primes | `Ideal.finite_minimalPrimes_of_isNoetherianRing` (`Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.MinimalPrime.Noetherian`) | 0 |
69	| Prime avoidance with one non-prime ideal (`𝔪²`) | `Ideal.subset_union_prime` (`Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Operations`) | 30 (apply step) |
70	| Krull intersection in a local ring | `Ideal.iInf_pow_eq_bot_of_isLocalRing` (`Mathlib.RingTheory.Filtration`) | 0 |
71	| `dim R/(x) + 1 = dim R` for `x ∈ 𝔪`, `x ∈ nonZeroDivisors R` | `ringKrullDim_quotient_span_singleton_succ_eq_ringKrullDim_of_mem_nonZeroDivisors` (`Mathlib.RingTheory.KrullDimension.Regular`) | 0 |
72	| `dim R/(x) + 1 = dim R` for `x ∈ 𝔪`, `x ∉ ⋃ minimalPrimes` (the joint-induction step *before* we know `IsDomain`) | `Module.supportDim_quotSMulTop_succ_eq_of_notMem_minimalPrimes_of_mem_maximalIdeal` (`Mathlib.RingTheory.KrullDimension.Regular`) — applied with `M = R`, with `Module.supportDim ↔ ringKrullDim` bridge | 30 |
73	| `R/(x)` is local + Noetherian | structural; immediate | 5 |
74	| `spanFinrank (𝔪/(x)) = spanFinrank 𝔪 - 1` for `x ∈ 𝔪 \ 𝔪²` (cotangent space dim drop) | **NO direct Mathlib lemma**. Closest is `IsLocalRing.finrank_cotangentSpace_eq_zero_iff` / `_le_one_iff` (qualitative); we need quantitative drop. Workable via `Submodule.finrank_quotient_add_finrank` applied to `(𝔪 + (x)/𝔪²)` ⊂ `CotangentSpace R`, but requires building a 1-dimensional κ-subspace from `x ∉ 𝔪²` | **~80 (substantial sub-helper)** |
75	| Joint-induction conclusion: assemble `IsRegularLocalRing (R/(x))` of dim `d-1` | `isRegularLocalRing_iff` (`Defs.lean:46`) | 15 |
76	| `(x)` prime ⟹ minimal prime in `(x)`: `(x)` has height ≥ 1 since `x ∉ ⋃ minimal primes`, hence chain `p₀ ⊊ (x)` with `p₀` minimal | `Ideal.height_le_one_of_isPrincipal_of_mem_minimalPrimes` + chain-extraction | 40 |
77	| Krull-intersection closing argument: `p₀ ⊆ (x) ⟹ p₀ = x · p₀ ⟹ p₀ ⊆ 𝔪ⁿ ⟹ p₀ = 0` | uses `Ideal.iInf_pow_eq_bot_of_isLocalRing` + `Nakayama` | 50 |
78	| Lift `(x :: rs')`-regular-sequence from `R/(x)` to `R` | `RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular.cons` (`Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.RegularSequence`) | 30 |
79	
80	**Total estimate**: ≈ 300 LOC, predominantly assembly. The single
81	non-routine sub-helper is the cotangent-space dim drop (≈ 80 LOC), which
82	is itself a worthwhile standalone result.
83	
84	- **Route (a) / (b)**: same proof tree — both go through the joint
85	  induction above. The directive's "(a) direct via minimal primes /
86	  Krull intersection" and "(b) regular-quotient induction" are not
87	  separate routes; they are the two halves of the same induction.
88	- **Route (c) via Serre's regularity (`pd_R(κ) = d`)**: requires
89	  `pd_R(κ) = d` for regular local `R`, which is harder than 00NQ
90	  itself (needs Koszul-complex-is-projective-resolution-of-κ, which is
91	  NOT in Mathlib at the pinned commit). **Out of scope.**
92	
93	- **Verdict**: Route (a)=(b) is the only feasible path, ≈ 300 LOC,
94	  multi-iter, with one substantial sub-helper (cotangent dim drop).
95	
96	### Decision 3: Alternative route via `IsRegularLocalRing.spanFinrank` — can we construct an `R`-regular sequence in `𝔪` without first proving `IsDomain`?
97	
98	Briefly: **no, not cleanly**, for the following reason.
99	
100	The first element `x₁` of the regular sequence must satisfy `IsSMulRegular

100	The first element `x₁` of the regular sequence must satisfy `IsSMulRegular
101	R x₁`, i.e. `x₁ ∈ nonZeroDivisors R`. By
102	`biUnion_associatedPrimes_eq_compl_nonZeroDivisors` (cite:
103	`Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.AssociatedPrime.Basic`), the non-zero-divisors
104	of a Noetherian `R` are exactly `R \ ⋃ Ass(R)`. So we need `x₁ ∈ 𝔪 \
105	(𝔪² ∪ ⋃ Ass(R))`.
106	
107	- `𝔪 ⊄ 𝔪²` from `spanFinrank ≥ 1` ✓.
108	- `𝔪 ⊄ p` for each *minimal* prime `p`: height(𝔪) = d > 0 = height(p) ✓.
109	- `𝔪 ⊄ q` for *embedded* primes `q ∈ Ass(R) \ minimalPrimes(R)`:
110	  **no a priori bound** — embedded primes can have any height between
111	  `1` and `d`. The standard fact "regular local ⟹ no embedded primes"
112	  is itself a corollary of `IsRegularLocalRing → IsDomain` (since a
113	  domain has Ass = {⊥}).
114	
115	Hence avoiding minimal primes alone is not enough to produce a
116	non-zero-divisor at step 1, and the induction cannot start without
117	the IsDomain step. The IsDomain detour is unavoidable.
118	
119	- **Verdict**: **NO_USEFUL_ALTERNATIVE**. The regular sequence
120	  existence and the IsDomain implication share the same induction;
121	  one cannot be cheaply isolated from the other.
122	
123	## Recommendation
124	
125	The gap is **genuine Mathlib-upstream content** (Stacks 00NQ has not
126	been formalized at pinned commit `b80f227`). Three actionable options
127	for the planner; the right call depends on iter-182's appetite for a
128	multi-iter sub-project versus a pivot.
129	
130	**Option 1 (recommended for iter-182): pivot Lane G prover.** Park
131	`exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` as a known multi-iter gap. Dispatch
132	the iter-182 Lane G prover to one of the two off-target depth lemmas
133	in the same file:
134	
135	- `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` (L228) — Stacks 00LP. The Ext
136	  characterization of depth. Substantive but independent of the
137	  `IsDomain` gap. Once closed, it becomes the canonical depth
138	  computation tool and is what `auslander_buchsbaum_formula`
139	  ultimately consumes.
140	- `depth_of_short_exact` (L268) — Stacks 00LE. The depth inequalities
141	  on a SES. Useful infrastructure even before `auslander_buchsbaum`
142	  lands.
143	
144	Both have well-defined Mathlib substrates (Ext groups in
145	`Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext`, short exact sequences in
146	`Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ShortComplex.ShortExact`) and are
147	self-contained relative to the `IsDomain` chain.
148	
149	**Option 2 (multi-iter sub-project, project-side): build 00NQ in
150	two lanes.**
151	
152	- *Sub-lane G1* (≈ 80 LOC): cotangent-space dim drop lemma. Statement:
153	  for `x ∈ 𝔪 \ 𝔪²` of a Noetherian local ring `R`, the cotangent
154	  space of `R/(x)` has dimension one less than that of `R`. Standalone
155	  and reusable.
156	- *Sub-lane G2* (≈ 200 LOC): assembly of the joint induction
157	  delivering both `IsRegularLocalRing R → IsDomain R` and
158	  `exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal`. Consumes G1.
159	
160	This is **the right long-term move** if A.4.a is on the critical path
161	and the Cohen-Macaulay instance is required this quarter. Estimate
162	2–3 iters with `helper budget ≤ 2` per iter.
163	
164	**Option 3 (Mathlib upstream PR, parallel).** Submit
165	`IsRegularLocalRing.toIsDomain` to Mathlib as a standalone PR (the
166	file `Mathlib.RingTheory.RegularLocalRing.Defs` is the natural home;
167	the proof needs ≈ 250 LOC and Mathlib reviewers will recognize the
168	00NQ pattern). Wait time is multi-week and outside the loop's
169	control. Worth doing in parallel with Option 1.
170	
171	**Project recommendation**: dispatch Option 1 this iter (pivot to
172	`depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`) and queue Option 2 as a new STRATEGY
173	row scheduled for iter-184+ once the off-target depth lemmas have
174	landed. Option 3 is a side action by the mathematician outside the
175	loop's prover budget. This sequencing preserves Lane G's iter-by-iter
176	substantive-content cadence and avoids a 2–3-iter dead lane on a
177	single sub-project.
178

6	import Mathlib
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Picard.FGAPicRepresentability
8	import AlgebraicJacobian.Genus
9	
10	/-!
11	# The identity component of the Picard scheme (A.3)
12	
13	This file is the **A.3** sub-build chapter for the project's positive-genus arm
14	of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It scaffolds the abstract identity-component
15	substrate for a `k`-group scheme locally of finite type and specialises it to
16	`G = Pic_{C/k}`, packaging:
17	
18	1. the open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure of the identity component,
19	2. the degree map `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`,
20	3. the abelian-variety identification of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` (the Jacobian variety
21	   of `C` when `C/k` is a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
22	   positive genus).
23	
24	## Status (iter-185 NEW file-skeleton)
25	
26	This file is the **iter-185 IdentityComponent** file-skeleton (NEW lane,
27	blueprint-reviewer HARD GATE cleared for the iter-184 plan-phase chapter
28	`Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`). Each of the five blueprint-pinned
29	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
30	`\lean{...}` pin in `Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`) with a `sorry` body. The
31	bodies are iter-186+ work; iter-185's mandate is the mechanical scaffold only.
32	
33	The 5 pinned declarations are:
34	
35	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent` (def, ~5 LOC) — the
36	   **identity component** `G^0` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite
37	   type, as a `k`-scheme (an `Over (Spec k)`-object). Abstract substrate
38	   reusable outside the Picard context; not yet in Mathlib.
39	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`
40	   (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the bundled statement that `G^0` is an open and
41	   closed subscheme of `G` via an open immersion `G^0 ↪ G`.
42	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme` (def, ~5 LOC) — the **identity
43	   component of the Picard scheme** `Pic⁰_{C/k}`, obtained by applying
44	   `IdentityComponent` to `G = PicScheme C`.
45	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree` (def, ~5 LOC) — the **degree
46	   map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`, extracting the leading coefficient of the
47	   Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed
48	   degree-one polarisation `O_C(1)`.
49	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
50	   — the **abelian-variety identification** of `Pic⁰_{C/k}`: smooth, proper,
51	   geometrically irreducible `k`-group scheme of dimension `g(C)` --- the
52	   Jacobian variety of `C`.
53	
54	## Note on type expressivity
55	
56	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
57	pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
58	
59	- `IdentityComponent G : Over (Spec (.of k))` — a `k`-scheme, not the
60	  tautological `G` itself.

113	
114	# Mathlib Analogist
115	
116	You are the read-only Mathlib-analogist subagent with **two modes**: API alignment (the default) and cross-domain inspiration. The directive's `## Mode` line selects which.
117	
118	## Mode: api-alignment
119	
120	The plan agent points you at a project declaration, a proposed definition, or a design question, and asks "what would Mathlib do, and is the project doing that?". Your job:
121	
122	1. Identify the actual design decisions at play (often multiple compressed into one question).
123	2. Find how Mathlib has resolved analogous decisions.
124	3. Compare the project's path to Mathlib's idiom.
125	4. Report whether the project should align with the idiom — and, if not, what the cost of the divergence is.
126	
127	You do **not** rubber-stamp the project's choice. If Mathlib has a canonical idiom and the project skipped it (most often because the prerequisite infrastructure looked hard), say so plainly. "It works" is not enough — the cost of API fragmentation, bridge lemmas, and code that can't compose with Mathlib is real.
128	
129	## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration
130	
131	The plan agent points you at a *structural problem* — a categorical, topological, algebraic, or analytic shape that's been resisting the project's current approach — and asks "has Mathlib solved this same shape somewhere else, possibly in a completely different mathematical field?". Your job:
132	
133	1. Distill the structural problem to its categorical / algebraic / topological core, stripping away the project's specific objects.
134	2. Search *broadly* across Mathlib for that shape. Cross domains aggressively — a sheaf condition on schemes shares structure with a sheaf condition on topological spaces; a descent argument in alg geom shares structure with a descent argument in functional analysis; a fixed-point setup in measure theory shares structure with order-theoretic Knaster–Tarski.
135	3. For each analogue you find, extract the *technique* used there (the tactic combination, the categorical lemma, the unbundling trick, the inductive step).
136	4. Map the technique back to the project's setting. State concretely what porting the technique would look like.
137	
138	You are explicitly NOT checking API alignment in this mode. The goal is to surface unfamiliar territory that may be borrowable, not to validate the project's existing definitions. Distant analogues are MORE valuable than nearby ones — the planner already knows the nearby ones.
139	
140	## Scope
141	
142	**One design question per invocation.** A design question may be broad ("how should we represent the Picard scheme?") or narrow ("instance-based vs. predicate-based"). Multiple Mathlib precedents in the SAME question are welcome. What you should NOT do is sprawl across unrelated decisions in one call.
143	
144	**Read-only.** You may read:
145	
146	- Project files (`.lean`, blueprint chapters, `references/`).
147	- Mathlib (via `archon-lean-lsp` MCP: `lean_leansearch`, `lean_loogle`, hover, signature lookup).
148	- Any existing `analogies/<slug>.md` summaries from prior calls.
149	
150	You may write:
151	
152	- `analogies/<slug>.md` — persistent design-rationale file future iters re-read.
153	- `.archon/task_results/mathlib-analogist-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path) — your report.
154	
155	You may **NOT** modify project source, blueprint, or any state file.
156	
157	## Directive Format
158	
159	The directive begins with `## Mode: api-alignment` or `## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration`. The remaining fields differ by mode.
160	
161	### Mode: api-alignment
162	
163	```markdown
164	# Mathlib Analogist Directive
165	
166	## Mode
167	api-alignment
168	
169	## Slug
170	<slug>
171	
172	## Design question
173	<the question. One question per directive.>
174	
175	## Project artifact(s) under question
176	- <file>:<lines> — <declaration or section>
177	- <file>:<lines> — <declaration or section>
178	
179	## Why now
180	<one or two sentences: what the dispatching agent is about to do (write, refactor, decide), and why a Mathlib precedent would inform it.>
181	
182	## Hints (optional)
183	<Mathlib namespaces, related concepts, or specific declarations the dispatcher suspects are relevant.>
184	
185	## Severity expectation
186	<one of: routine | high-stakes>
187	- routine: cheap sanity check on a small choice
188	- high-stakes: this design will be load-bearing; be strict about idiom adherence
189	```
190	
191	### Mode: cross-domain-inspiration

195	
196	## Mode
197	cross-domain-inspiration
198	
199	## Slug
200	<slug>

206	sheaf condition for a locally trivializable functor of modules over
207	a site whose covering family is finite flat" rather than "we need a
208	sheaf condition for quasi-coherent modules on Spec(O_K)".>

# Mathlib Analogist Directive

## Mode
api-alignment

## Slug
quotscheme-isbasechange-tilde

## Design question

How does Mathlib idiomatically prove `IsBaseChange` for **sections of a
module-sheaf pullback over a pair of affine opens** — i.e. given
`g : Y ⟶ X` a morphism of schemes, `N : X.Modules`, `U : Y.Opens` and
`V : X.Opens` both affine opens with `U ≤ g ⁻¹ᵁ V`, prove that the
canonical `Γ(X, V)`-linear map
`Γ(N, V) →ₗ[Γ(X, V)] Γ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj N, U)`
exhibits `Γ((pullback g).obj N, U)` as `Γ(Y, U) ⊗_{Γ(X, V)} Γ(N, V)` —
**without first requiring `N` to be quasi-coherent**?

Specifically:

- Does Mathlib's `Tilde` API + `Scheme.Modules.pullback` machinery have
  a *direct* `IsBaseChange`-conclusion lemma we can invoke?
- If `[IsQuasiCoherent N]` is the missing hypothesis, what does the
  Mathlib idiom look like, and where in the consumer chain
  (`pullback_app_isoTensor_isBaseChange` consumer
  `canonicalBaseChangeMap_app_app_isIso_of_isAffineOpen_of_isAffineBase`
  at L656) should the quasicoherence assumption be threaded?
- Is `Module.Flat.isBaseChange` (algebraic Stacks 02KE on the affine
  ring map `g.appLE V U e`) the cheaper route, and if so, what
  flatness hypothesis would the consumer signature carry?
- Are there other Mathlib-canonical idioms — e.g. via
  `PresheafOfModules.pullback`, or via
  `AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocallyOfFiniteType` + flat-base-change
  cohomology infrastructure — that bypass both the `IsQuasiCoherent`
  and the explicit `Module.Flat` hypothesis?

## Project artifact(s) under question

- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean:563-584` —
  `pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap_isBaseChange` (typed sorry; the
  iter-186 Step 4 typed sorry that gates the Step 2 `baseMap`
  closure cascade).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean:508-545` —
  `pullback_app_isoTensor_baseMap` (axiom-clean iter-186; the
  `Γ(X, V)`-linear baseMap built from `pullback_app_isoTensor_unitAtV`
  + presheaf restriction `Y.presheaf.map (homOfLE e).op`).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean:586-606` —
  `pullback_app_isoTensor_isBaseChange` (axiom-clean modulo the
  Step 4 sorry above).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean:608-660` —
  `pullback_app_isoTensor` (the affine-open section formula iso —
  the consumer of the IsBaseChange Prop).
- `AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean` consumer
  `canonicalBaseChangeMap_app_app_isIso_of_isAffineOpen_of_isAffineBase`
  L656 — the inline sorry the planner's iter-187 plan wants to
  close via the Step 4 cascade.

## Why now

The iter-187 progress-critic (`route187`) returned a **CHURNING**
verdict on Lane F with the explicit corrective: "Mathlib-idiom
consult on `IsBaseChange` + `IsQuasiCoherent` + `Tilde` functor
before another prover round". The Step 4 `IsBaseChange` sorry has
been the named blocker across iter-184/185/186 (3 consecutive
PARTIAL iters) and the project has been proposing the
"Tilde-isoTop route" for 3 iters without execution. Sorry count
stuck at 9 across iter-183-iter-186.

We need this verdict BEFORE assigning a Lane F prover this iter:
either confirm the Tilde-isoTop route is correct (with
`[IsQuasiCoherent N]` threaded), or surface an alternative
(`Module.Flat.isBaseChange` or other Mathlib idiom) that the
prover can execute.

The downstream blocker for the project is the consumer
`canonicalBaseChangeMap_app_app_isIso_of_isAffineOpen_of_isAffineBase`
(L656 inline sorry) which propagates to
`flatBaseChangeCohomology` and ultimately to
A.2.b.iii Quot scheme construction (STRATEGY.md row A.2.b.iii
Iters left ~36-72; elapsed ~16). This is on the critical path
for the Route A engine.

## Hints (optional)

Relevant Mathlib namespaces and concepts:

- `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules` (the `.Modules` category =
  `SheafOfModules` over the scheme's structure ring).
- `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde` — the `~` functor from
  modules over a commutative ring to module-sheaves on `Spec`.
  Specifically: `AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde.isoTop`,
  `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Modules/Tilde.lean` L71-77 (the iter-186
  task_result referenced the pattern at this line range).
- `AlgebraicGeometry.IsQuasiCoherent` — the quasicoherence
  predicate (Mathlib already has this at b80f227).
- `Module.Flat.isBaseChange` /
  `LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.IsBaseChange` (the algebraic
  Stacks 02KE statement on ring-map flatness).
- `AlgebraicGeometry.PresheafOfModules.pullback` (presheaf-level
  pullback, BEFORE sheafification).
- `IsBaseChange.equiv` and `IsBaseChange.of_lift_isoStep` (the
  consumer-facing equiv-from-base-change-prop API).
- `Scheme.Modules.pullback : (X ⟶ Y) → Y.Modules ⥤ X.Modules`
  (Mathlib backbone, used iter-186 Lane A.1.b for the
  LineBundlePullback closures).

The iter-186 task_result for `QuotScheme.lean` documents (lines
68-87 of the task_result, archived to
`.archon/iter/iter-186/`):

> "1. **Tilde route**: on `Spec(Γ(X, V))` (via `hV.isoSpec`) and
>    `Spec(Γ(Y, U))` (via `hU.isoSpec`), `N|_V` restricts to a
>    tilde-shape sheaf — requires either:
>    - quasi-coherence of `N` (currently not assumed at the
>      consumer signature; iter-187 plan-agent decision: add
>      `[IsQuasiCoherent N]` hypothesis to the consumer chain and
>      propagate), or
>    - the alternative route via `Module.Flat.isBaseChange`
>      (algebraic Stacks 02KE) on the affine ring map
>      `g.appLE V U e`."

We need you to assess whether either path is the Mathlib idiom or
whether a third (e.g. via `PresheafOfModules.pullback` +
`Sheafify` + an existing Mathlib base-change-of-tilde lemma)
better matches Mathlib's design.

## Severity expectation

high-stakes — the choice between `[IsQuasiCoherent N]` thread vs.
`[Module.Flat]` thread vs. third route propagates back through the
consumer chain
`canonicalBaseChangeMap_app_app_isIso_of_isAffineOpen_of_isAffineBase`
→ `flatBaseChangeCohomology` → A.2.b.iii consumers. Getting this
wrong wastes another 3-iter cycle on the same wall.
